comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about psycho-visual metrics
comments.quote: With significantly fewer parameters and a versatile architecture, we report competitive results confirmed by objective and psycho-visual metrics, illustrated by visualizations.
comments.explanation: The paper states that results are confirmed by 'psycho-visual metrics,' but the experimental section (Section 5) reports only FID, PSNR, and NIQE — none of which are psycho-visual metrics. No user study or perceptual evaluation is conducted. This claim is false as written. Remove 'psycho-visual metrics' or add a proper perceptual study to support it.

comments.title: M_n definition not representative of gradient
comments.quote: $M_{n}=\prod_{i=n-1}^{1}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\left(f^{i}(x)\right)=\prod_{i=n-1}^{1}\left(\frac{\partial\text{res}}{\partial x}\left(f^{i}(x)\right)+\text{Id}\right),$ (6)
comments.explanation: The matrix $M_n$ is defined as the product from $i=n-1$ down to $1$, which corresponds to the factor multiplying $\frac{\partial f}{\partial w}(x)$ in the (incorrect) gradient formula (5). In the correct gradient expression (a sum over $j$), each term has a product from $i=n-1$ down to $j+1$, so $M_n$ is only one of many factors. The claim that "the length of the SGD steps on $w$ depends on the eigenvalues of $M_n$" is therefore not justified, because the gradient is a sum of terms with different product ranges. The authors should clarify that $M_n$ is a worst-case factor (largest product) and justify why its eigenvalues dominate the gradient norm, or redefine $M_n$ to reflect the full sum.

comments.title: FID values for Horse→Zebra direction inconsistent
comments.explanation: For the Horse→Zebra direction, PoL/CycleGAN achieves FID 53.0, which is better than CycleGAN's 89.7. However, for the reverse direction (Zebra→Horse), PoL/CycleGAN gives 112.3, which is worse than CycleGAN's 110.5. This asymmetry is not discussed. The authors should explain why PoL improves one direction but degrades the other, as this raises questions about the method's consistency.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of universal approximation theorem
comments.quote: More precisely, the theorem states that stacking a number of basic blocks can approximate any function with arbitrary precision, provided it has enough hidden units, with mild conditions on the non-linear basic blocks.
comments.explanation: The universal approximation theorem (Hornik et al., 1989) applies to a single hidden layer network with enough width, not to stacking many blocks. The paper conflates depth (stacking layers) with width (hidden units per layer). The theorem does not guarantee that stacking a fixed block repeatedly (as in PoL) can approximate any function — that is a different claim about iterative function systems. Rewrite to accurately state that the theorem concerns a single hidden layer with sufficient width, and separate the discussion of iterative function systems (Barnsley) as a distinct line of work.

comments.title: Contradiction between simplicity and cited state-of-the-art complexity
comments.quote: s proposal is in sharp contrast with the trend of current state-of-the-art works to specialize the architecture and to increase its complexity and number of parameters *(Fu et al., 2019; Viazovetskyi et al., 2020; Choi et al., 2020)*.
comments.explanation: The paper claims PoL is simpler than state-of-the-art works, but the cited references (Fu et al., Viazovetskyi et al., Choi et al.) are not discussed or compared anywhere else in the paper. The paper only compares against CycleGAN and NiceGAN, which are older and simpler baselines. This creates a mismatch: the paper criticizes a trend it does not engage with. Either add comparisons to these cited methods or remove the claim about being in contrast to current state-of-the-art.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for CoGAN
comments.quote: When there is a shared latent space between the domains, a possible choice is to use a variational auto encoder like in CoGAN Liu & Tuzel (2016).
comments.explanation: The paper cites CoGAN (Liu & Tuzel, 2016) as an example of using a variational autoencoder for unpaired image translation. However, CoGAN is not a variational autoencoder; it is a coupled GAN framework that learns a joint distribution over two domains by sharing weights in the generator and discriminator, without any VAE component. The correct reference for a VAE-based shared latent space approach is UNIT (Liu et al., 2017), which explicitly couples a VAE with a GAN. This misattribution may mislead readers about the lineage of the proposed method.

comments.title: Incorrect description of progressive learning methods
comments.quote: Progressive learning and inference time modulation are performed in multi-scale methods such as SinGAN *(Rott Shaham et al., 2019)* and ProgressiveGAN *(Karras et al., 2017)*.
comments.explanation: The paper groups SinGAN and ProgressiveGAN together as examples of progressive learning with inference time modulation. However, SinGAN is a single-image GAN that learns a pyramid of generators at different scales, but it does not support inference time modulation (i.e., stopping early to trade off quality for speed). ProgressiveGAN (Karras et al., 2017) progressively grows both the generator and discriminator during training, but at inference time it uses the full-resolution model; it does not allow early stopping to modulate output. The claim that these methods perform 'inference time modulation' is inaccurate. The correct examples for inference-time early stopping are the works cited later (Huang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2017; Veit and Belongie, 2017).

comments.title: Missing stride and kernel size specifications
comments.quote: The encoder $\mathrm{Enc}:I\rightarrow\mathcal{E}$ produces the embedding and consists of two convolutions. The decoder $\mathrm{Dec}:\mathcal{E}\rightarrow I$ turns the embedding back to image space and consists of two transposed convolutions.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify the stride, kernel size, padding, or number of channels for the convolutions and transposed convolutions. Without these details, the claim that the embedding has spatial resolution 64×64 and 256 channels is not verifiable. For example, if the first convolution uses stride 1, the resolution would not reduce by a factor of 4. The authors should add explicit architectural parameters (e.g., 'Conv2d(3, 128, kernel=4, stride=2, padding=1) followed by Conv2d(128, 256, kernel=4, stride=2, padding=1)') to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Linearization ignores nonlinear dynamics
comments.quote: When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation $L\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}$ (i.e., ignoring the normalization and the ReLU non-linearity), we have $M_{n}=(L+\operatorname{Id})^{n-1}$.
comments.explanation: The analysis replaces the nonlinear residual block (with ReLU and normalization) by a fixed linear map $L$. Under the true dynamics, the Jacobian $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ is input-dependent: ReLU introduces a diagonal mask that varies with the activation, and normalization layers scale/offset the activations. Consequently, the eigenvalues of $M_n$ under the true dynamics can differ substantially from $(\lambda_i+1)^{n-1}$, especially because ReLU can zero out gradients (vanishing) and normalization can amplify them. The claim that eigenvalue explosion motivates progressive training is based on this linearization, which may not hold in practice. The authors should acknowledge this limitation and provide empirical evidence (e.g., gradient norm plots) to support the claimed instability.

comments.title: Progressive training justification is speculative
comments.quote: This approach avoids the numerical exponentiation effects at initialization mentioned in the previous paragraph. After the warm-up phase, the gradient descent starts to converge, so these numerical effects become unlikely.
comments.explanation: The claim that progressive training avoids numerical exponentiation effects is not supported by any formal analysis or empirical evidence in this section. The referenced 'previous paragraph' (Section 3.2) discusses exponentiation in the context of a linearized residual block, but the actual network includes nonlinearities and normalization layers that could alter the dynamics. The statement that gradient descent convergence makes numerical effects 'unlikely' is vague and unquantified. The authors should either provide a rigorous argument (e.g., bounding the spectral norm of the Jacobian during warm-up) or present training loss/gradient norm curves to substantiate the claim. Without such support, the justification remains speculative.

comments.title: Adaptive stopping criterion lacks specification and validation
comments.quote: Stopping criterion at inference time. Each image of domain  $\mathcal{A}$  is more or less close to domain  $\mathcal{B}$ . For example, when denoising, the noise level can vary so the denoising strength should adapt to the input. Similarly for horse  $\rightarrow$  zebra: a white horse is closer to a zebra than a brown horse. Therefore, at inference time, we can adjust the number of compositions as well. In particular, for each test image, we select  $n$  that best deceives the discriminator, thus effectively adapting the processing to the relative distance to the target domain.
comments.explanation: The proposed adaptive stopping criterion is described only qualitatively. The paper does not specify how 'best deceives the discriminator' is operationalized (e.g., argmax of discriminator logit, threshold on probability, or some other rule). Moreover, no quantitative comparison is provided between fixed-n and adaptive-n inference on any metric (PSNR, FID, etc.), nor is there an analysis of the distribution of selected n values across test images. Without such evidence, the claim that the method 'effectively adapts the processing' is unsubstantiated. The authors should add a precise description of the selection procedure and report ablation results (e.g., average PSNR/FID for adaptive n vs. fixed n) to validate the approach.

comments.title: Training for modulation lacks formal justification
comments.quote: Training for modulation. If the network is trained with a fixed number of compositions, the intermediate states do not correspond to modulations of the transformation that "look right" (see Appendix A). Therefore, in addition to this scheduled number of iterations during the first  $n$  epochs of warm-up, we also randomize the number of iterations in subsequent epochs. This forces the generator to also produce acceptable intermediate states, and enables modulating the transform.
comments.explanation: The claim that training with a fixed number of compositions yields intermediate states that do not 'look right' is referenced to Appendix A, but the main text provides no formal definition of what constitutes a 'correct' intermediate state or how randomization addresses the issue. The mechanism by which randomizing the number of iterations during training forces acceptable intermediate states is not explained. Without a theoretical or empirical demonstration (e.g., showing that intermediate outputs under randomized training are perceptually plausible or satisfy some invariance property), this justification remains heuristic. The authors should either provide a formal argument (e.g., showing that randomization encourages the generator to produce outputs that are consistent across different numbers of compositions) or present qualitative/quantitative evidence from Appendix A in the main text.

comments.title: FID comparison lacks statistical rigor
comments.quote: We compare the Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of CycleGAN, NiceGAN and our approach applied to this method in Figure 4. The FID measures the similarity between two datasets of images, we use it to compare the target dataset with the transformed dataset. It is a noisy measure for which only large deviations are significant. Yet the results and visualization show that our method has results comparable to those of CycleGAN and NiceGAN, achieved with much fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that FID is noisy and only large deviations are significant, yet they report only point estimates without any measure of variance (e.g., standard deviation, confidence intervals) or statistical significance tests. Without such measures, the claim that results are 'comparable' is not supported. The authors should report FID with error bars (e.g., bootstrapped confidence intervals) and, if possible, perform a significance test (e.g., Mann-Whitney U) to substantiate the claim of comparable performance.

comments.title: High-resolution results lack quantitative evaluation
comments.quote: Figure 5 (left) depicts results obtained with our method trained on high resolution images.
comments.explanation: The high-resolution experiments are presented only as visual examples (Figure 5) with no quantitative metric such as FID, LPIPS, or user study scores. The paper claims PoL can handle high-resolution images where CycleGAN struggles, but provides no numerical evidence to support this. The authors should compute FID on the high-resolution test sets (e.g., for horse→zebra at 512×512 or higher) and compare with a CycleGAN baseline trained on patches, as described in the text.

comments.title: Composition in embedding space claim unsupported
comments.quote: The composition in the embedding space gives better results than decoding/encoding to image space mid-way.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that composing transformations in the embedding space 'gives better results' but provides no quantitative evaluation or comparison to composing in image space. Only a single visual example (Figure 8) is shown. Without a metric (e.g., FID, LPIPS) or a user study, this claim is unsubstantiated. The authors should compute FID or a perceptual distance for both composition methods on at least two task combinations (e.g., denoising then deblurring, or horse→zebra then summer→winter) to support the claim.

comments.title: Missing ablation for weight sharing on unpaired tasks
comments.quote: We compare the Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of CycleGAN, NiceGAN and our approach applied to this method in Figure 4.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that weight sharing (iterating the same block) is beneficial, but the only comparison against independent blocks is in Table 1, which is limited to denoising with PSNR. For the central unpaired translation tasks (horse→zebra, summer→winter, etc.), no such ablation is provided. Without this comparison, a reader cannot tell whether the performance of PoL comes from weight sharing or simply from having a residual block in the latent space. The authors should repeat the unpaired translation experiments (e.g., horse→zebra and summer→winter) with independent blocks using the same total number of residual iterations, and report FID and visual results.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about discriminator adjustment at inference
comments.quote: While in most examples the discriminator is only used for training, Powers of layers can also exploit it to adjust the transformation to the input image at inference time.
comments.explanation: The paper does not provide any experimental results or formal description of how the discriminator is used at inference time to adjust the transformation. Section 5 and the figures show only training-time discriminator use. Without a demonstration or ablation, this claim is unsupported. Remove the claim or add a dedicated experiment showing inference-time discriminator adjustment with quantitative results.

comments.title: Vague claim about composing transformations without evidence
comments.quote: The flexibility offered by the common embedding space allows the modulation of the transformation strength, or to compose several transformations.
comments.explanation: The paper provides only a single visual example of composition (Figure 8) without any quantitative metric (e.g., FID, LPIPS) or comparison to composing in image space. The claim that composition in the embedding space is beneficial is not substantiated. Add quantitative results for at least two task combinations (e.g., denoising then deblurring) comparing composition in embedding space versus image space.

comments.title: No statistical significance or variance reported
comments.quote: We test on the Urban-100 (Huang et al., 2015) dataset. Unless specified otherwise, we set the number of compositions to  $n_{\mathrm{te}} = n_{\mathrm{tr}}$  and measure the Peak Signal to Noise Ratio (PSNR) of our model on the dataset images, degraded with the same intensity as at training time.
comments.explanation: The paper reports PSNR and NIQE values as single numbers without any measure of variance (e.g., standard deviation, confidence intervals) across the test set. Given that Urban-100 contains 100 images, the reported values could be sensitive to outliers or random variation. The authors should report standard deviations or confidence intervals to allow readers to assess the reliability of the comparisons.

comments.title: NIQE metric not defined or cited for deblurring
comments.quote: For the JPEG case we use the Naturalness Image Quality Evaluator metric (NIQE, lower=better) instead, because it is more sensitive to JPEG artifacts (Mittal et al., 2013).
comments.explanation: The paper uses NIQE only for JPEG deblocking, but the tables in Figure 3 include NIQE columns for denoising and deblurring as well (e.g., 'NIQE' columns under 'Impact of the amount of training data'). The text does not explain why NIQE is used for those tasks or whether it is a suitable metric. The authors should clarify the metric choice for each task and cite relevant references.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about fixed autoencoder weights
comments.quote: We start from an image autoencoder architecture with fixed weights. For each task we learn a residual block operating in the latent space, which is applied iteratively until the target domain is reached.
comments.explanation: The abstract states that the autoencoder has 'fixed weights' but does not specify how those weights are obtained or why they are fixed. If the autoencoder is pre-trained on a large dataset (e.g., YFCC100M as mentioned later), the claim that it is 'fixed' is accurate, but the abstract omits this crucial detail. Without this context, a reader might assume the autoencoder is randomly initialized and frozen, which would likely produce poor results. The abstract should briefly state that the autoencoder is pre-trained on a large corpus and then frozen, to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Vague claim about performance comparison
comments.quote: Experimentally, we show that the performance of our model is comparable or better than CycleGAN and Nice-GAN with fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims performance is 'comparable or better' than two baselines, but this is too vague to be meaningful. The paper later reports FID on a few datasets, but the abstract does not specify which tasks or metrics support this claim. Moreover, comparing only to CycleGAN and Nice-GAN is insufficient given the maturity of the field (see paper-level issues). The abstract should either state the specific tasks and metrics where PoL outperforms, or qualify the claim more precisely (e.g., 'on denoising and deblurring tasks, PoL achieves higher PSNR with fewer parameters').

comments.title: Overclaim on performance parity with CycleGAN and NiceGAN
comments.quote: On various tasks, It gives similar performance to CycleGAN and NiceGAN with fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The paper's quantitative results (Figure 4) show that PoL achieves FID values that are sometimes better and sometimes worse than CycleGAN and NiceGAN, but the comparison is limited to only these two baselines. The claim of 'similar performance' is not supported by statistical significance tests or confidence intervals, and the paper does not compare against more recent state-of-the-art methods (e.g., UNIT, MUNIT, CUT). Without a broader comparison and error bars, the claim overstates the evidence. Rewrite as: 'On the tasks evaluated, PoL achieves FID scores comparable to CycleGAN and NiceGAN while using fewer parameters, though further comparison with a wider range of methods is needed.'

comments.title: Missing mention of progressive training schedule
comments.quote: A specific training schedule is required to alleviate the exponentiation effect of the iterations.
comments.explanation: The abstract mentions a 'specific training schedule' but does not name it or explain it. The paper later calls this 'progressive training,' which is a key contribution. The abstract should explicitly use the term 'progressive training' and briefly describe it (e.g., 'we progressively increase the number of iterations during training'). This would give readers a clearer sense of the method's novelty.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a 'Powers of Layers' architecture for image-to-image translation that iterates a single residual block in a fixed autoencoder latent space. The core idea of modulating transformation strength via iteration count is conceptually interesting and aligns with recent trends in efficient generative modeling. However, the manuscript contains methodological inconsistencies regarding evaluation protocols, ambiguous optimization claims, and missing computational benchmarks that weaken the overall contribution.

The proposed iterative residual block offers a parameter-efficient alternative to standard deep generators, and the progressive training schedule addresses a known weight-sharing challenge. The authors successfully demonstrate competitive FID scores and improved denoising performance with fewer parameters. Nevertheless, the paper requires substantial clarification on its evaluation setup, stopping criterion logic, and empirical validation of its optimization claims to meet publication standards.

Overview issues:

- Inconsistent framing of unpaired training and paired evaluation: Section 4 explicitly states the objective is to remain in a 'completely unpaired setting during the training phase,' yet the denoising experiments rely on PSNR computed against ground-truth images from Urban-100. While evaluating restoration methods on paired test data is standard practice, the paper's insistence on an unpaired framing creates confusion about the supervision signal available at test time. If the method is truly unpaired, metrics like NIQE or user studies should be prioritized, or the evaluation protocol should be explicitly labeled as 'unpaired training with paired testing.' The authors should clarify this distinction and align their claims with the actual experimental setup to avoid misleading readers about the method's applicability to strictly unpaired scenarios.

- Ambiguous and mathematically inconsistent stopping criterion: Section 3.3 proposes using the discriminator to select the optimal number of compositions $n$ at inference time. However, the text claims the discriminator should 'maximize the target discriminator error,' which directly contradicts the standard GAN minimax objective where the generator seeks to maximize $D_B(G(a))$ (i.e., make the output probability approach 1). If the discriminator error is truly maximized, the generator would be pushed to produce images that look like the source domain, not the target. The authors need to correct this phrasing, provide the exact optimization objective used for $n$ selection, and justify why this criterion reliably correlates with perceptual quality rather than simply overfitting to the discriminator's decision boundary.

- Strong assumption of a fixed, task-agnostic autoencoder latent space: The architecture fixes the encoder and decoder weights after pre-training on YFCC100M, assuming this single latent space suffices for all downstream translation tasks. This assumption is highly restrictive and lacks empirical validation. Different domains (e.g., natural photographs versus artistic paintings) often require distinct feature hierarchies and semantic alignments. The paper does not ablate the impact of the pre-training dataset, nor does it discuss how the fixed latent space handles domain-specific structures that the autoencoder might compress or discard. Adding an ablation study comparing the fixed autoencoder against a jointly trained or domain-specific encoder would clarify the robustness and limitations of this design choice.

- Missing computational trade-off analysis beyond parameter count: The authors heavily emphasize the reduced parameter count (15.9M for PoL versus 28.2M for CycleGAN) as a primary advantage. However, inference latency and FLOPs scale linearly with the number of compositions $n$, which can reach 30 or more during training and adaptive inference. Without reporting inference speed, memory bandwidth requirements, or FLOPs, the practical utility of the 'fewer parameters' claim remains unverified for real-world deployment. The revision should include a computational benchmark table detailing FLOPs, inference latency across different $n$ values, and memory usage to provide a complete picture of the efficiency gains.

- Insufficient empirical validation of the progressive training hypothesis: Section 3.2 derives an eigenvalue argument suggesting that weight sharing causes optimization instability due to the term $(	ilde{	heta}_i + 1)^{n-1}$, and Section 3.3 introduces a progressive warm-up schedule to mitigate this. Despite this theoretical motivation, the paper provides no empirical evidence, such as gradient norm trajectories, loss curves, or convergence comparisons, to demonstrate that the progressive schedule actually resolves the claimed instability. Standard weight-sharing or residual initialization techniques often converge without such schedules. The authors should add convergence diagnostics or a direct comparison with alternative initialization strategies to substantiate the necessity of their progressive training approach.

- Lack of analysis on iterative feature interference and mode collapse: Iterating a single residual block repeatedly risks compounding errors, feature interference, or mode collapse as the latent representation is transformed step-by-step. The paper does not analyze how feature distributions shift across iterations or measure sample diversity beyond aggregate FID scores. High iteration counts could theoretically amplify minor biases in the residual block, leading to unrealistic or repetitive textures. Including an analysis of intermediate feature statistics, sample variance across different $n$, or a comparison of diversity metrics against standard deep generators would strengthen the claim that iterative composition is a viable alternative to increasing network depth.

- Missing toy model validation for progressive training claim: The paper derives an eigenvalue argument in Section 3.2 to explain why weight sharing causes optimization instability and proposes a progressive warm-up schedule to fix it. However, the authors never test this mechanism on a simple, fully-specified model. A low-dimensional vector example would let readers verify that the $(\lambda_i + 1)^{n-1}$ term actually causes divergence without the schedule and that the warm-up stabilizes convergence. Without this calibration, the theoretical motivation remains abstract and disconnected from the empirical results. I recommend adding a 1D or 2D simulation where a single linear or ReLU block is iterated, plotting gradient norms and loss curves with and without the progressive schedule to demonstrate the claimed stabilization effect.

- Underdeveloped inference-time stopping criterion: The abstract and Section 3.3 claim the discriminator automatically determines the optimal iteration count $n$ at test time. The text provides no algorithmic details on how this selection is performed. Is the system performing a discrete grid search over $n \in [1, n_{tr}]$ for every test image? Is it using gradient-based optimization on a relaxed integer variable? How are the discriminator scores aggregated across a batch? Without specifying the search procedure and its computational overhead, the claim of an adaptive mechanism is incomplete and cannot be reproduced. The authors should explicitly define the optimization routine for $n$, report the additional latency it introduces, and compare it against a fixed-$n$ baseline using the same search budget.

- Missing analysis of iterative feature interference and stability: Iterating a single residual block repeatedly risks compounding approximation errors or pushing the latent representation into regions where the fixed autoencoder decoder fails. The paper reports aggregate FID and PSNR scores but does not analyze how the feature distribution shifts across iterations or where the method breaks down. High iteration counts could theoretically amplify minor biases in the residual block, leading to unrealistic textures or mode collapse. The authors should include an analysis of intermediate feature statistics or sample diversity metrics across different $n$ to validate that the iterative composition remains stable. A plot of latent space variance or nearest-neighbor distances over iterations would clarify whether the progressive training successfully prevents destructive feature interference.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an interesting architectural idea but suffers from contradictory claims regarding unpaired versus paired evaluation, an ambiguous stopping criterion, and a lack of empirical validation for its core optimization claims. Addressing these methodological gaps and providing computational trade-off analysis is necessary before publication.

Revision targets:

- Clarify the evaluation protocol by explicitly distinguishing between unpaired training and paired testing, and align metric choices (PSNR versus unpaired metrics) with the stated experimental setup.

- Correct the mathematical description of the discriminator-based stopping criterion and provide empirical justification for its reliability across different noise levels and domains.

- Add convergence diagnostics (e.g., gradient norms, loss curves) to empirically validate the progressive training schedule and demonstrate its necessity over standard initialization methods.

- Include a computational benchmark reporting FLOPs, inference latency, and memory usage across varying composition counts to substantiate the efficiency claims.

- Analyze feature distribution shifts and sample diversity across iterations to address potential mode collapse or feature interference inherent in weight-sharing iterative architectures.

comments.title: Contradictory stopping criterion objective
comments.quote: selects the value  $n_{\mathrm{te}}$  maximizing the target discriminator error for a given i
comments.explanation: In standard GAN formulations, the discriminator outputs a probability that an input is real. Maximizing the error on generated images means minimizing that probability, which would select the iteration where the output looks most fake. The authors intend to pick the iteration where the image looks most realistic, which requires minimizing the discriminator error or maximizing the discriminator score. This phrasing directly contradicts the stated goal and will confuse readers attempting to reproduce the adaptive inference mechanism.

comments.title: Identity loss norm deviates from standard CycleGAN baseline
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Id}}(G_{AB},G_{BA})=\mathbb{E}_{b\sim\mathcal{B}}[\|G_{AB}(b)-b\|_{2}]+\mathbb{E}_{a\sim\mathcal{A}}[\|G_{BA}(a)-a\|_{2}].$
comments.explanation: The authors state they use the same losses as CycleGAN, yet the identity loss here uses an L2 norm. The original CycleGAN formulation defines identity reconstruction with an L1 norm. This substitution alters the penalty landscape for reconstruction errors and breaks the claimed equivalence to the standard baseline. If the L2 norm is an intentional architectural choice, the claim of using the same losses must be revised.

comments.title: Conflation of small eigenvalues and spectral radius decrease
comments.quote: wever, to decrease $(\lambda_{i}+1)^{n-1}$ sufficiently, $\lambda_{i}$ must be so small that it introduces floating-point cancellations when the residual block is added back to the shortcut connection.
comments.explanation: The eigenvalue argument contains a contradiction. For small positive $\lambda_i \approx 0$, the term $(\lambda_i+1)^{n-1} \approx 1$ and actually grows with $n$, rather than decreasing. To strictly decrease the magnitude below 1, $\lambda_i$ must be negative and sufficiently large in magnitude. Meanwhile, floating-point cancellation in $x + \text{res}(x)$ occurs precisely when the residual is tiny relative to $x$, which corresponds to $\lambda_i \approx 0$. The regime that causes cancellation is exactly the regime where the powered term fails to decrease. The authors incorrectly link precision loss in the shortcut addition with the spectral radius of the Jacobian product.

comments.title: Inconsistent product limits between Jacobian and $M_n$
comments.quote: $\frac{\partial f^{n}}{\partial x}(x)\ =\ \prod_{i=n-1}^{0}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\big{(}f^{i}(x))\quad\text{and}\quad\frac{\partial f^{n}}{\partial w}(x)\ =\ \prod_{i=n-1}^{1}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\left(f^{i}(x)\right)\frac{\partial f}{\partial w}(x).$ (5)
comments.explanation: Equation (5) correctly applies the chain rule for $n$ compositions, yielding a product of $n$ Jacobian factors for $\frac{\partial f^n}{\partial x}$. However, Equation (6) defines $M_n = \prod_{i=n-1}^{1} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}(f^i(x))$, which contains only $n-1$ factors. The text then states that SGD step length depends on the eigenvalues of $M_n$, but stability analysis requires the full Jacobian $\frac{\partial f^n}{\partial x}$, which has $n$ factors. This off-by-one discrepancy means $M_n$ does not represent the complete sensitivity of the network output to the input.

comments.title: Unsupported claim on warm-up and numerical effects
comments.quote: This approach avoids the numerical exponentiation effects at initialization mentioned in the previous paragraph. After the warm-up phase, the gradient descent starts to converge, so these numerical effects become unlikely.
comments.explanation: The assertion that gradient descent convergence makes numerical exponentiation effects unlikely lacks theoretical or empirical support. Exponentiation effects arise from the forward-pass composition of shared weights, which is a structural property of the network rather than a consequence of optimization dynamics. Progressive warm-up may delay the manifestation of these effects by keeping $n$ small initially, but it does not eliminate them, nor does convergence render them improbable. The authors should clarify the mechanism behind this claim or provide ablation studies comparing convergence behavior with and without the warm-up schedule.

comments.title: Unvalidated discriminator-based stopping criterion
comments.quote: In particular, for each test image, we select  $n$  that best deceives the discriminator, thus effectively adapting the processing to the relative distance to the target domain.
comments.explanation: The inference-time stopping criterion relies entirely on the discriminator's output without establishing that its score reliably correlates with the optimal transformation strength. In GANs, discriminator probabilities often saturate or become unreliable for intermediate states, meaning the maximum score may not correspond to the perceptually or metric-optimal $n$. The authors should validate this assumption by plotting discriminator scores against ground-truth metrics across different $n$, or justify why the discriminator's decision boundary aligns with the desired transformation level.

comments.title: Memory usage claim conflates compute with memory
comments.quote: In contrast, our proposal can adapt the memory used by changing its number of compositions, so we can apply it to very large images without running out of memory.
comments.explanation: This statement incorrectly attributes memory adaptation to the number of compositions. In a fully convolutional network with weight sharing, peak memory consumption is dictated by the input resolution, batch size, and feature map channels. Varying the sequential depth $n$ scales the FLOPs and inference latency linearly, but does not reduce the memory footprint. Readers will be confused about the actual efficiency gains if compute and memory are conflated.

comments.title: Missing inference algorithm for adaptive iteration count
comments.quote: Adjusting  $n_{\mathrm{te}}$  at inference time. Each image is more or less distant from the target domain, so we explore adapting the transformation to each image rather than applying a fixed transformation. For example, depending on the amount of noise, we may want to adjust the strength of the denoising. By modulating  $n_{\mathrm{te}}$  we can adapt the transformation to each image. Figure 6 shows that the more noisy the input image is, the more we should compose to best denoise with Powers-of-Layers.
comments.explanation: The section introduces an adaptive inference strategy but completely omits the mechanism for selecting $n_{\mathrm{te}}$. The abstract and introduction explicitly state that a discriminator determines the optimal iteration count, yet this section provides no algorithmic details, thresholds, or search procedure. Readers cannot reproduce the adaptive behavior without knowing how discriminator scores are aggregated or how the discrete iteration count is chosen. This omission breaks the internal consistency of the paper's methodology description.

comments.title: Contradictory claim regarding inference-time discriminator usage
comments.quote: While in most examples the discriminator is only used for training, Powers of layers can also exploit it to adjust the transformation to the input image at inference time.
comments.explanation: The conclusion presents the inference-time discriminator usage as an operational feature of the method, yet the technical sections explicitly lack the algorithmic specification required to implement this capability. Claiming the method can exploit the discriminator for inference-time adjustment contradicts the paper's own methodology, which provides no search routine, optimization objective, or computational analysis for this step. This overstates the method's current capabilities relative to what is actually implemented.

comments.title: Vague performance claim lacks evaluation metrics
comments.quote: Experimentally, we show that the performance of our model is comparable or better than CycleGAN and Nice-GAN with fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The claim that the model is comparable or better than established baselines is too vague for an abstract. Readers cannot assess the validity of this statement without knowing which metrics and on which datasets the comparison holds. Specifying the evaluation protocol grounds the performance claim and allows immediate comparison with the literature.

comments.title: Unclear terminology regarding training schedule and exponentiation effect
comments.quote: A specific training schedule is required to alleviate the exponentiation effect of the iterations.
comments.explanation: The phrase exponentiation effect is non-standard and ambiguous in this context. It likely refers to the compounding of approximation errors or gradient instability caused by repeatedly applying the same weight matrix, but the abstract does not clarify this. Additionally, a specific training schedule is left undefined. Briefly naming the schedule and explicitly stating what phenomenon it mitigates will improve clarity.

comments.title: Mismatched citation for image restoration
comments.quote: cal{B}$. Therefore we do not have access to any parallel data *(Conneau et al., 2017)*, which is a realistic scenario in many applications, e.g., image restoration.
comments.explanation: The cited work addresses cross-lingual sentence representation learning in natural language processing, not image restoration or unpaired image translation. Citing an NLP paper here creates confusion and misrepresents the literature. Replace this citation with a foundational computer vision reference for unpaired translation, such as Zhu et al. (2017) on CycleGAN or Liu et al. (2017) on UNIT, which formally establish the unpaired image-to-image setting.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of fractal literature
comments.quote: c blocks. Studies on non-linear complex holomorphic functions involved in escape-time fractals showed that iterating simple non-linear functions can also construct arbitrarily complex landscapes *(Barnsley et al., 1988)*. These f
comments.explanation: The description conflates two distinct mathematical constructs. Escape-time fractals like the Mandelbrot set arise from iterating complex polynomials, whereas Barnsley et al. (1988) focuses on Iterated Function Systems that rely on contractive affine mappings, not holomorphic functions. The authors should either correct the description to accurately reflect IFS theory or cite a different reference if they intend to discuss holomorphic iteration.

comments.title: Misattribution of StyleGAN2 citation and capabilities
comments.quote: n unpaired learning. Other approaches such as the Fader networks (Lample et al., 2017) or StyleGan2 (Viazovetskyi et al., 2020) act on scalar or boolean attributes that are disentangled in the latent space (eg., age for face images, wear glasses or not, etc). Nevertheless, this results in complex models, for which dataset size and the variability of
comments.explanation: The citation for StyleGAN2 is incorrect; the foundational StyleGAN2 paper is authored by Karras et al. (2020), not Viazovetskyi et al. Furthermore, the description mischaracterizes StyleGAN2's core contribution. StyleGAN2 does not primarily rely on disentangling scalar or boolean attributes for controllable translation. Instead, it introduces a mapping network and adaptive style injection to decouple high-level semantic features from low-level stylistic details. The authors should correct the citation and accurately contrast StyleGAN2's architectural innovations with the attribute-disentanglement approach of Fader Networks.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of early-exit and dynamic inference methods
comments.quote: The inference time of some architectures can be modulated by stopping the forward pass at some layer *(Huang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2017; Veit and Belongie, 2017)*.This differs from our approach, where the number of residual block compositions (“powers”) can be chosen, to shorten the inference.
comments.explanation: The cited works do not support the claim that they modulate inference time by truncating the forward pass at intermediate layers. Huang et al. (2016) introduced DenseNet, emphasizing dense connectivity, while Wu et al. (2017) and Veit & Belongie (2017) provided foundational empirical analyses of ResNet architectures. None of these papers propose dynamic inference or early-exit mechanisms. The authors should replace these citations with appropriate references on dynamic inference or early exiting, and clarify how their proposed layer-power mechanism conceptually differs from established dynamic computation strategies.

comments.title: Domain mismatch in residual block quantifier
comments.quote: $f_{\mathcal{AB}}(x)=x+\operatorname{res}_{\mathcal{AB}}(x),\forall x\in\mathcal{A}.$ (2)
comments.explanation: The quantifier $\forall x \in \mathcal{A}$ implies the residual block operates on raw images, but the text explicitly states all translation operations take place in the fixed embedding space $\mathcal{E}$. The derivation chain breaks because the block processes feature maps, not raw images. The quantifier must be updated to reflect the actual input space of the block.

comments.title: Contradictory description of encoder composition
comments.quote: The encoder and decoder consist of 2 layers and a residual block. The embedding space $\mathcal{E}$ of our model is $256\times 64\times 64$:
comments.explanation: The opening sentence claims the residual block is a component of both the encoder and decoder. However, the subsequent sentences explicitly define the encoder as having only two convolutions and the decoder as having only two transposed convolutions. The residual block is introduced later as a distinct translation module between the encoder and decoder. This structural contradiction misleads readers about the model's actual topology and which components are frozen during pre-training.

comments.title: Ill-defined iteration of domain-indexed block
comments.quote:
In contrast to these fixed architectures, PoL iterates the $f_{\mathcal{AB}}$ block $n\geq 1$ times, which amounts to sharing the weights of a deeper network:

$G_{\mathcal{AB}}(x)=\operatorname{Dec}(f_{\mathcal{AB}}^{n}(\operatorname{Enc}(x))),\forall x\in\mathcal{A}.$ (4)
comments.explanation: The notation $f_{\mathcal{AB}}$ explicitly indexes the source domain $\mathcal{A}$ and target domain $\mathcal{B}$. If $f_{\mathcal{AB}}$ maps embeddings from $\mathcal{E}_\mathcal{A}$ to $\mathcal{E}_\mathcal{B}$, then the composition $f_{\mathcal{AB}} \circ f_{\mathcal{AB}}$ is only mathematically well-defined if the output space of the first application matches the input space of the second. The text mentions that intermediate representations are compatible, but it does not clarify whether $\mathcal{E}_\mathcal{A} = \mathcal{E}_\mathcal{B}$ or if the iteration implicitly switches to $f_{\mathcal{BA}}$ after the first step. Without this clarification, the definition of $f_{\mathcal{AB}}^n$ is ambiguous.

comments.title: Undefined parameter K in model size table
comments.explanation: The parameter count table introduces the variable K in the residual block column without defining it anywhere in the text. The accompanying paragraph states that PoL iterates a single residual block an arbitrary number of times, but does not specify the fixed K used for the reported 15.9M count. Furthermore, the factor of 2 multiplying K × 1.1M is unexplained. Add a definition for K and clarify the coefficient to ensure the parameter count is reproducible.

comments.title: FID metric dismissal contradicts comparability claim
comments.quote: It is a noisy measure for which only large deviations are significant. Yet the results and visualization show that our method has results comparable to those of CycleGAN and NiceGAN, achieved with much fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: Dismissing FID as noisy while simultaneously claiming comparability to baselines creates a logical gap. If the metric is unreliable for small differences, the assertion that the methods are comparable lacks quantitative support. The text should either report the exact FID values to demonstrate that differences fall within the reported noise margin, or explicitly acknowledge that the visual results drive the comparability claim rather than the metric.

comments.title: Misattribution of modulation capability to embedding space
comments.quote: The flexibility offered by the common embedding space allows the modulation of the transformation strength, or to compose several transformations.
comments.explanation: The conclusion attributes the ability to modulate transformation strength directly to the common embedding space, but this capability actually stems from the iterative composition mechanism (varying the number of residual block applications, $n$), not the embedding space itself. The shared latent representation enables the composition to be well-defined across domains, but the modulation of strength is a function of the iteration count. This misattribution obscures the actual source of the method's flexibility.

comments.title: Modulation and composition claims lack formal support
comments.quote: On various tasks, It gives similar performance to CycleGAN and NiceGAN with fewer parameters. The flexibility offered by the common embedding space allows the modulation of the transformation strength, or to compose several transformations.
comments.explanation: The formal results section only defines the adversarial, cycle consistency, and identity losses, along with their coefficients. It does not introduce a common embedding space, nor does it describe mechanisms for modulating transformation strength or composing transformations. The discussion asserts these capabilities as direct consequences of the method, but the provided formal setup does not establish how or why these operations are enabled. To align the discussion with the formal results, either remove these specific claims or add a brief explanation of how the defined generators and losses facilitate modulation and composition.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes iterating a residual block in the latent space of a fixed autoencoder for unpaired image-to-image translation. While the results are promising, the manuscript contains a critical logical error in the description of the stopping criterion, employs a methodologically unfair comparison against CycleGAN regarding parameter count, and fails to provide quantitative benchmarks against its closest competitor, GANHopper. Additionally, the evaluation of the denoising task relies on paired metrics (PSNR), which contradicts the paper's framing as a general unpaired method.

This work presents an interesting architectural modification to CycleGAN that offers control over transformation strength and potential parameter efficiency. The core idea of weight sharing through iteration is sound and well-motivated by the universal approximation theorem. However, the experimental validation is currently undermined by a contradiction in the stopping criterion logic, a comparison that favors the proposed method purely on expressiveness rather than parameter efficiency, and a lack of rigorous comparison with the most relevant prior work. The paper would benefit significantly from correcting these logical and methodological gaps.

Overview issues:

- Discriminator Stopping Criterion Contradiction: Section 3.3 states that at inference time, the method selects the number of iterations $n_{te}$ that 'best deceives the discriminator.' However, Section 4 explicitly describes this process as 'maximizing the target discriminator error.' In a standard GAN setup, the generator aims to minimize the discriminator's error (or loss) to make the image appear real. Maximizing the error implies the discriminator is most confident that the image is fake, which is the opposite of the intended effect. The paper must clarify whether it intends to minimize the error (or maximize the log-probability) to deceive the discriminator, as the current phrasing is internally contradictory and would lead to selecting the worst possible transformation if interpreted literally.

- Unfair CycleGAN Parameter Comparison: The paper claims that Powers of layers (PoL) achieves comparable or better performance with 'fewer parameters' than CycleGAN (Section 4). However, the comparison is methodologically flawed because CycleGAN typically utilizes a fixed depth of 9 residual blocks, while PoL iterates a single residual block up to 30 times. Comparing a deep, highly expressive model (CycleGAN) to a shallow model with high expressiveness via iteration (PoL) on the metric of total parameter count is misleading; it merely demonstrates that a shallow network with many iterations can be more parameter-efficient than a deep network with fewer iterations. A fair comparison would isolate the weight-sharing effect by comparing CycleGAN with 1 block to PoL with 1 block, or by explicitly stating the trade-off between expressiveness and parameter count.

- Missing Quantitative Comparison with GANHopper: The Related Work section (Section 3) identifies GANHopper (Lira et al., 2020) as a competitor because it iterates CycleGAN generators. However, the Experiments section (Section 5) provides no quantitative comparison with GANHopper. The paper only lists qualitative differences (e.g., 'they have to use additional discriminators'). Without a head-to-head numerical comparison, the claim that PoL is 'comparable or better' than the closest existing approach in this specific domain is unsubstantiated. The authors should include a quantitative comparison (e.g., FID scores) between PoL and GANHopper on the standard CycleGAN benchmarks to validate their contribution against this specific prior art.

- High-Resolution Memory Efficiency Claim: The paper claims that 'our proposal can adapt the memory used by changing its number of compositions, so we can apply it to very large images without running out of memory' (Section 5). This claim is technically incorrect if the number of iterations $n$ is large. Memory usage scales linearly with the number of forward passes (iterations). Figure 6 shows that for high noise levels, the optimal number of iterations $n_{te}$ can be as high as 30. Therefore, applying PoL to high-resolution images with high noise would require significant memory, potentially exceeding that of a standard CycleGAN. The paper needs to address this trade-off, either by demonstrating that $n$ remains small for high-resolution images or by acknowledging that the memory advantage is conditional on the number of iterations required for a given task.

- Inconsistent Evaluation of Denoising Task: The paper frames itself as a method for 'unpaired image-to-image translation,' yet its primary quantitative analysis (Section 4) focuses on denoising tasks using Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) against the original ground-truth image. While the training is unpaired, the evaluation metric (PSNR) relies on the availability of the original image, effectively making it a paired evaluation. In contrast, the comparison with CycleGAN is evaluated using FID (Fréchet Inception Distance), which measures distribution similarity without ground truth. Comparing PSNR (pixel-level accuracy) to FID (distribution-level realism) is methodologically inconsistent. The authors should either evaluate denoising using an unpaired metric (e.g., FID against the target domain) or explicitly clarify why PSNR is the appropriate standard for this specific task despite the 'unpaired' framing.

- Demonstrating failure of baselines under varying noise conditions: The paper argues that the ability to modulate transformation strength is essential for handling unknown noise levels, yet it does not demonstrate a failure mode of standard CycleGAN when faced with such variation. CycleGAN is typically trained on a fixed noise distribution, so applying it to images with significantly different noise intensities often leads to hallucinations or artifacts rather than accurate denoising. The current experiments compare PoL against CycleGAN on fixed noise levels, leaving the specific advantage of adaptive strength unproven. The authors should provide a specific example where CycleGAN fails to denoise an image with a noise level outside its training distribution, while PoL successfully recovers the original image, thereby validating the practical necessity of the proposed method.

- Concrete mathematical toy model for optimization dynamics: Section 3.2 provides a mathematical derivation of the optimization instability caused by weight sharing, relying on the eigenvalues of a linearized residual block. While the derivation is rigorous, readers lack an intuitive grasp of the mechanism without a concrete example. The paper would benefit from a simple toy model, such as a 1D signal or a 2D grid, where the authors can explicitly compute the gradients for different numbers of iterations. This would allow readers to visually and numerically verify the 'exponentiation effect' described in the text and understand why progressive training is required to mitigate it.

- Quantitative analysis of transformation composition: Section 5 introduces the capability to compose multiple transformations (e.g., denoising followed by deblurring) by freezing intermediate blocks, but the analysis is purely qualitative. It is unclear from the current text whether this composition strategy offers a significant performance advantage over using a single, larger transformation block or stacking networks in image space. To develop this implication, the authors should quantify the performance of the composed approach against a single-block baseline on a specific task, such as removing both Gaussian noise and blur simultaneously, to demonstrate the practical value of this feature.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The paper presents a novel and interesting approach to image-to-image translation with valuable properties like modifiable transformation strength. However, the current manuscript contains a critical logical error in the stopping criterion description, a methodologically unfair comparison against CycleGAN, and lacks essential quantitative validation against the closest competitor, GANHopper. These issues prevent the work from being accepted in its current form and require substantial revision to ensure rigor and clarity.

Revision targets:

- Correct the stopping criterion logic in Section 3.3 and Section 4 to ensure it minimizes the discriminator's error (or maximizes the log-probability) rather than maximizing it.

- Add a quantitative comparison between PoL and CycleGAN where both use the same number of residual blocks (e.g., 1 block vs 1 block) to isolate the benefit of the proposed weight-sharing mechanism.

- Include a numerical comparison (e.g., FID scores) between PoL and GANHopper on the standard CycleGAN benchmarks to validate the contribution against the most relevant prior work.

- Clarify the high-resolution memory trade-off in Section 5, explicitly stating whether the number of iterations $n$ remains small enough to provide a memory advantage for high-resolution inputs.

- Re-evaluate the denoising task metrics in Section 4, either by using an unpaired metric like FID or by more clearly distinguishing between the 'unpaired training' and 'paired evaluation' aspects of the experiments.

comments.title: Discriminator Stopping Criterion Contradiction
comments.quote: select  $n$  that best deceives the discriminator
comments.explanation: Section 3.3 states that at inference time, the method selects the number of iterations $n_{te}$ that 'best deceives the discriminator.' However, Section 4 explicitly describes this process as 'maximizing the target discriminator error.' In a standard GAN setup, the generator aims to minimize the discriminator's error (or loss) to make the image appear real. Maximizing the error implies the discriminator is most confident that the image is fake, which is the opposite of the intended effect. The paper must clarify whether it intends to minimize the error (or maximize the log-probability) to deceive the discriminator, as the current phrasing is internally contradictory.

comments.title: Missing Quantitative Comparison with GANHopper
comments.quote: NHopper by Lira et al. (2020) proposes to iterate CycleGAN generators in order to perform the transformation. However, their method has many differences with
comments.explanation: The Related Work section identifies GANHopper as a competitor because it iterates CycleGAN generators, yet the Experiments section provides no quantitative comparison with this specific prior art. The paper only lists qualitative differences (e.g., 'they have to use additional discriminators'). Without a head-to-head numerical comparison (e.g., FID scores) between PoL and GANHopper on standard benchmarks, the claim that PoL is 'comparable or better' than the closest existing approach in this domain is unsubstantiated.

comments.title: High-Resolution Memory Efficiency Claim
comments.quote: our proposal can adapt the memory used by changing its number of compositions, so we can apply it to very large images without running out of memory
comments.explanation: This claim is technically incorrect if the number of iterations $n$ is large. Memory usage scales linearly with the number of forward passes (iterations). Figure 6 shows that for high noise levels, the optimal number of iterations $n_{te}$ can be as high as 30. Therefore, applying PoL to high-resolution images with high noise would require significant memory, potentially exceeding that of a standard CycleGAN. The paper needs to address this trade-off, either by demonstrating that $n$ remains small for high-resolution images or by acknowledging that the memory advantage is conditional on the number of iterations required for a given task.
